  • William Sonoma - $2.64 to $3.04 = 15.15% increase.
  • Watsco - $12.00 to $13.20 = 10.00% increase.

Final Thoughts

To reiterate, the objective of this investable universe is to help me identify which of the screened stocks may be an opportune investment at the moment. While all 50 of these stocks are potentially high-quality businesses, it makes more sense to focus on the companies that have stronger track records, appear reasonably or attractively valued, and present a desirable future rate of return.

While this list focuses on dividend-paying stocks, the primary objective is on total return. My investing strategy focuses on achieving a lucrative long-term total return while also accumulating a growing passive dividend stream along the way.

data.title: Fund Flows In Focus: Investors Eye Commodity ETFs Amid Middle East Conflict
data.description:
Market swings and oil price surges are rattling investors and driving a shift toward stability. Andres Rincon, Director and Head of ETF Sales and Strategy at TD Securities, says fund flows indicate investors are looking to commodity and defensive ETFs for protection and diversification.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Market volatility has picked up amid the continued conflict in the Middle East. But where are investors looking for safety amid all the uncertainty? Joining us now to discuss where the funds are flowing is Andrés Rincon, Managing Director and Head of ETF Sales and Strategy with TD Securities. Andrés, welcome back to the program.

Andrés Rincon: Thank you for having me.

Greg Bonnell: These are interesting times. Obviously, people are trying to make investment decisions based on a lot of unknown variables. When it comes to fund flows in the ETF world, what are you seeing amid all this?

Andrés Rincon: First and foremost, we're still seeing a lot of money go into the ETF space. As you put on the chart earlier, the S&P 500 has had a pretty good year to start. So we're still seeing a lot of money go into ETFs in general.

If you look outside of ETFs, a lot of money is still flowing in to some degree. But it's a little bit different than what we see in ETF land. In ETF land, it's still slightly skewed towards equities, which would say it's risk-on. But I'll talk a little bit more about the intricacies of that.

But if you look outside of ETFs, it's been a lot of money going into actually fixed income, so a flight to safety. So a bit of a divergence there between what you see in ETF land and bonds or flows in general.

Now in ETFs, specifically, yes, if you look at flows, it's been very focused on equity ETFs. But it's not the same as last year. You've seen a lot of money going into energy ETFs, in materials ETFs. So commodities, in general, have been very, very popular. And also value ETFs, which include many of the same names that I just mentioned. So a slightly different tilt to what we saw last year.

And this is not just in the US. In Canada or Canadian ETF, we've seen the same thing. We've seen a lot of money go into equity in themselves, but also a lot of focus on Canadian-focused equities versus a US, for example. So, actually, the two largest areas of focus are Canadian equities and international equities, not US equities, which is what we've seen historically for Canadian investors.

If you look at fixed income now, a lot of the money has focused on the short end of the curve. Basically, people don't want to play on the long end, with the war and inflation and all that might be happening there. So everybody's staying on the short end of the curve and in agg and in investment grade, in general. Not a lot of money going into high-yield bonds in general.

But what's really been interesting this year is commodities. As I mentioned on the equities side, we've seen decent flow going into gold ETFs, broad commodity ETFs, silver and oil ETFs, especially broad commodity ETFs. We've seen quite a bit of money go into those ETFs, both in Canada and in the US.

And what we see there really is, regardless of what happens with the war, we do see an inflationary environment. And the war might end tomorrow or the day after tomorrow. But with the disruption of trade and many other commodities being higher, we do see inflation creeping up. And there's a big interest in playing that space via commodity ETFs.

Greg Bonnell: Do we think that is a safety play then, that people are saying, OK, the traditional 60/40 portfolio, they've had questions about it, particularly after 2022? Are commodities seen as another safer part of the portfolio?

Andrés Rincon: What I will say is not necessarily safer because it does have quite a lot of volatility in today's date, but an offset to the risks that are underlying other parts of the portfolio, like just broad equities, for example. So if somebody has a lot of exposure to the S&P 500 and whatnot, this is viewed right now as a hedge from inflation and political uncertainty.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about, so this is what investors are doing in this climate. What about new ETFs out there? Every time you come on, there's always some new product.

Andrés Rincon: It's very exciting. We see a lot of new things every year and every month. And since I've been here, there's been a couple of new developments. Just hot off the press today, there was an ETF filing in Canada for covered call commodity ETFs. Just to be clear, there are some covered call gold ETFs, for example. So BMO has one here in Canada. But there aren't any that are broad market.

And so Global X in Canada just filed for a broad commodity covered call ETF, also a slightly leveraged version of that, and also for silver ETFs and a covered call version for uranium. So the idea is that they're trying to cover all the commodity space, obviously, given all the interest, with an enhanced version in a cover called version.

Another really fascinating area is prediction markets. You've obviously heard -- everybody has heard quite a bit about prediction markets over the last few years or months. More recently, the regulators in Canada, the OSC, allowed a direct investing platform to actually, in the future, offer prediction markets. And because of that growth, we've seen now a filing in the US for prediction market ETFs.

Greg Bonnell: How would that even work? I'm trying to wrap my brain around that.

Andrés Rincon: Yeah, no, it's an interesting filing. And just to be clear, I am not sure that this one specifically will get through from an approval point of view. But, basically, what it's doing is, at this point, it's just political predictions. So it will be whether the US government or the next president will be Republican or Democrat, whether the Senate will be Republican or Democrat.

So in this case, it would be four ETFs that, choose one of those. And the value of the ETF, similar to prediction markets, will be derived by the probability of this event happening. So now this has been a filing, once again.

And it's very interesting that it's been filed in the US, because in Canada, although it's been approved recently by the OSC for non-financial markets for two direct investing platforms now, historically the OSC has said that this is a binary market and they did not like binary products. So it is interesting to see or we're interested to see where this goes in the US and in Canada.

Greg Bonnell: Definitely things to keep in mind there, too. When you talked about the commodity ETFs and the covered calls, I imagine there the thing to note is probably the same risk when you're a covered call equity ETF, you're going to miss gold explode. You're going to miss out on some of that upside.

Andrés Rincon: That's right. And, actually, some of the other gold commodity ETF here in Canada actually is a call spread. So it actually gives you some yield with still some upside.

This one from Global X just filed, so I haven't had a chance to look through all of them. But it might be in the underlying stocks or in the underlying commodity. I suspect it's in the underlying stocks, which would be very similar outcome as if you just bought a normal broad market covered call ETF.

Greg Bonnell: Now, in an environment like this, where there's a lot of risk and we can see some big moves in markets from day to day, some investors may be looking at leveraged products. What do they need to keep in mind if they start looking at leveraged products to try to play a market like this?

Andrés Rincon: So, right now, it's very tempting to get into these products. And what I will say is that these are very well-established vehicles for trading purposes, not necessarily for investing purposes. These products are designed to provide daily exposure, so daily 2x, or minus or negative, or inverse exposure, or 3x exposure. They're not designed for long-term holds. That's the first thing I'll say.

Now in volatile times, some people might be tempted to go and buy an oil ETF, a 2x oil or an inverse oil, and so on and so forth. And if you time it right, it might work out. But it's important to understand that this is not a long-term play. These are, once again, just for short-term holds.

Now, with that in mind, what these funds basically do is you give them cash, and then they will invest in a swap that will give you two times long exposure or inverse, and so on and so forth. So they enhance the return, be it positive or negative, of that fund.

Now, one of the features of this ETF is that they are path dependent. They depend very much on how many times the ETF goes up or how many times the ETF goes down. And because of compounding in these ETFs and the daily return of the ETF, actually, really the long-term performance of the ETF is very much impacted by the volatility. And, right now, we have a lot of it.

So this is a time where, if you hold this ETF over a long period of time, it will, generally speaking, underperform the long-term expectations of what one would have on a 2x fund, for example. So it's important that if you are getting into these ETFs, you hold them just for a day. They're just for trading. They're not for long-term holding.

data.title: An Autonomous Vehicle Investment Roadmap
data.description:
It’s been a while since I last updated my AV Investment Roadmap, and a lot has changed. In this article, I’ll provide some necessary background on autonomous vehicle technologies and markets and then explain the landscape for the first and most important market: autonomous ride-hailing services (robotaxis) in the US. In subsequent articles, I’ll explain the investment landscape for Chinese companies, long-haul trucking, and other AV markets.

Background to Understanding the AV Investment Landscape

For any investor, there are three important interrelated concepts to understand about autonomous vehicles and their potential. The first is the different categories of autonomous driving. It’s critical to understand that the categories are not progressions; they represent distinct technical capabilities for different markets. The second is the alternative technology platforms for autonomous driving. There is not just one approach. Third, autonomous vehicles are not a single market. There are several with different opportunities, competitors, and technologies.

Categories of Autonomous Driving

The following chart summarizes the four categories of autonomous driving with the SAE levels and my descriptions. The SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) established the recognized framework for categorizing levels of autonomous driving in 2014 and updated it in 2016.

Level 0 is traditional driving with no autonomous capabilities, and Level 1 is basic functions like cruise control.

  • Semi-Autonomous Driving (SAE Level 2) – The combination of adaptive cruise control, lane centering, and automatic braking is necessary at this level. This is what millions of drivers currently use, primarily for autonomous highway driving. Using this semi-autonomous capability, the driver must remain constantly attentive and ready to regain immediate control of the vehicle, which is why these systems monitor drivers’ paying attention. Tesla is currently rated at this level.
  • Substantially Autonomous Driving (SAE Level 3) – This is a more advanced version of Level 2, adding features such as the ability to turn corners, stop at traffic signals, and adjust to speed limits. Drivers still need to be attentive, but don’t need to take control immediately, so the vehicle still needs a complete set of driver controls (steering wheel, brakes, mirrors, etc.). There are very few vehicles operating at this level, and few locations where it’s permitted. In general, there is increasing recognition that this is not practical at a large scale, with a market of people willing to pay for this capability. Personal vehicles are the only realistic market for this level since the driver needs to be in the vehicle. Tesla FSD can function at this level (I use it this way all the time); however, it is only authorized as a Level 2 vehicle. I think that Level 2 systems will eventually migrate to Level 3, but there may not be a large market for premium pricing.
  • Sufficiently-Autonomous Driving (SAE Level 4) – This is the primary level for the new autonomous vehicle markets because sufficiently-autonomous driving does not require a driver or driver controls. Autonomous ride-hailing (robotaxi), autonomous long-haul trucking, autonomous delivery, and other autonomous vehicle markets function at this level. The most important characteristic here is that the vehicles are restricted to geofenced areas using high-definition maps, so they don’t need to be capable of driving autonomously everywhere (which is why they are called sufficiently-autonomous). For example, they are unable to open gates at gated communities, so for the time being, they just restrict access to those locations. These AVs are provided in approved metropolitan areas or along approved long-haul truck routes and are managed as fleets. Although I need to acknowledge that Tesla’s strategy is inconsistent with this.
  • Fully-Autonomous Driving (SAE Level 5) – At this level, an AV can drive anywhere with no restrictions. It does not require a driver or driver controls. This is the level of aspiration of Tesla FSD and what will be required for its robotaxi strategy, but I don’t see this level as feasible for many years. No matter how capable FSD is, the robotaxi will just stop and get stuck when it reaches a gated community.

Alternative Technology Platforms

There are multiple variations of autonomous-driving technology platforms. I compare four primary platforms in the chart that follows using the most important elements of the autonomous driving technologies.

The most important distinction is between Tesla and the others. In the long-standing argument about technology, Tesla does not use lidar or radar (anymore). Most important, in my view, is the lack of HD map use. These maps are critical for enabling geofencing and providing semantic data, such as exact lane boundaries (even when road lines are missing or blurred), traffic signs, and unique intersection restrictions, including the thousands of variations of right-on-red restrictions.

I need to add that there is still another autonomous driving technology platform not used in the US. China has begun integrating autonomous-driving infrastructure technologies into its autonomous-driving systems. This can provide critical information to AVs about locations, traffic status, and other useful details, even outside the AV’s field of view. This shifts much of the computing and mapping burden from the AV, enabling them to be simpler and less expensive. I’ll discuss this more in a subsequent article on the Chinese AV market.

The dominant Chinese AV architecture relies on smart infrastructure and more centralized control in order to reduce vehicle costs. Baidu's recent systemic fleet failure in Wuhan highlights this correlated risk.

AV Markets

As I previously mentioned, AVs are not a single market. Investors need to view autonomous vehicles as distinct markets. There are several primary ones:

  • U.S. Autonomous Ride-Hailing (Robotaxi) – This is the largest current market opportunity for AVs. Autonomous vehicles, primarily from Waymo, are already beginning to replace ridesharing in early markets. I’ll discuss this in detail later in the article.
  • Chinese Autonomous Ride-Hailing (Robotaxi) – These US and Chinese markets are distinct because government restrictions effectively prevent Chinese companies from entering the US market, and vice versa. Since Chinese companies trade on American stock markets, investors should understand what is happening there.
  • Autonomous Long-Haul Trucking – This market is just emerging this year. It will also be a considerably large market, which I will discuss in a subsequent article. The key concept enabling this market is the emergence of the transfer hub model, in which autonomous trucks take the trailer from one transfer hub to another, while a traditional truck driver brings and takes the trailer to and from the local destination.
  • Autonomous Home Delivery – In this market, much lower-cost autonomous delivery vehicles will replace home delivery from delivery companies such as DoorDash and Domino’s. Initially, this will emerge more slowly, following autonomous ride-hailing, but it will accelerate as smaller, customized delivery vehicles further reduce costs.
  • Specialty Autonomous Vehicles - This market includes autonomous shuttles, farming vehicles, construction equipment, military vehicles, and other specialty vehicles.
  • Autonomous Vehicles Components - The previously listed are end markets; however, there is also a market for a variety of systems and components used in autonomous vehicles, such as lidar sensors and computer systems.

Autonomous Ride-Hailing (Robotaxi) Market

Now, let’s look at the first market: autonomous ride-hailing services (ARS), also referred to as robotaxis. Competition in this market has changed substantially. Some promising large companies, such as Ford (F), GM (GM), and Apple (AAPL), have dropped out, as well as some insufficiently funded startups. The market requires substantial capital, first to develop the technology and then to finance deployment. Two deep-pocketed technology companies have risen to the top.

There are two fundamental ARS market dynamics that investors must understand. The first is the industry structure: autonomous ride-hailing combines different services and technologies, which can be best understood as a set of layers with different revenue streams. The second important point to note is that the potential market for autonomous ride-hailing services is huge.

Ride-Hailing Industry Structure

The ride-hailing industry is a platform structured with multiple layers, each providing a segment of the total service. Some companies provide all layers of the service, while others vary it by metropolitan area, like Waymo. Still others, like Uber (UBER), focus solely on one layer of the service. This is one of the most critical factors for investors to understand, as revenue in this market will be distributed across these layers.

The following chart describes the autonomous ride-hailing platform in detail, but I’ll just focus on the primary layers.

There are five primary layers:

  • The Ride Request and Dispatch Layer – This is typically the app where a customer requests a ride and the provider dispatches the autonomous vehicle for pickup. As illustrated, this layer comprises several elements. This is what Uber currently does, dispatching its human drivers to fulfill customer trip requests, and it is what Uber will do in its autonomous ride-hailing service. Uber takes a fee of approximately 28%-30% of the fare for its ridesharing business, but it remains to be seen what share it gets of the autonomous ride-hailing business, and whether that will be sufficient as the price per trip declines.
  • The Fleet Management Layer – This is the nitty-gritty of maintaining the AV fleet in each metropolitan area. Companies are selectively outsourcing some labor-intensive functions while retaining control over critical safety activities. An example of this is Waymo in Miami, where it partners with Moove. Moove is providing vehicles and many of these services in this layer for Waymo, in addition to fleet financing. Uber’s new Autonomous Solutions business may also provide these services to others.
  • The Fleet Ownership/Leasing/Financing Layer – ARS vehicles require a substantial capital investment, and it’s still an open question who will own and lease them and what the financial structures will be. Uber has made significant investments in companies developing ARS vehicles that are expected to be part of its fleet. Moove is also providing some vehicle financing. There may also be the creation of different financing approaches, such as REIT-like structures.
  • The Vehicle Layer – I separate this into the vehicle itself and the autonomous driving technology (next). Currently, first-generation vehicles are in use. These are modified versions of personal vehicles with the autonomous technology adapted for them. They are very expensive but necessary to deploy the technology initially. Waymo, for example, uses the Jaguar I-PACE. The next generation ARS vehicles are specifically designed for autonomous ride-hailing services. They don’t have driver controls, have more passenger space, and are much less expensive. Waymo’s soon-to-be-released Waymo Ojai is an example. It’s a purpose-built ARS vehicle developed by Zeekr. The Tesla Cybercab is another example.
  • The Autonomous Technology Layer – This is the most important layer and what I call the Defining Technology of the AV platform. It includes the sensors, computer systems, software, and AI that are all integrated to make the vehicle autonomous. The Waymo Driver and Tesla FSD are examples. In some cases, this layer is supplied separately, as with Nuro supplying the technology for the Uber partnership.

The revenue and profitability of companies competing in the autonomous ride-hailing services (ARS) market depend on the layers in which they operate, and investors need to understand this clearly.

The Potential Autonomous Ride-Hailing Services Market

The ARS market is just emerging, so while there are several forecasts of market size, there is little consistency among them. For example, The Business Research Company projects that it will be $33.49 billion by 2030. Ark Invest forecasts $300-$600 billion by 2030 and $4 trillion in annual platform revenue by 2035.

I'll look at it this way. ARS will displace ridesharing as we know it today. I estimate the US ridesharing market in 2025 to be $62-$74 billion, growing approximately 20% annually. (Note: I get to this estimate by taking Lyft's disclosed North American, primarily US, bookings and extending it by its market share to the total market.)

There is already some proof that ARS is penetrating the ridesharing market. As of November 2025, Waymo was taking significant market share from Uber and Lyft, gaining 27% share in just 20 months.

Currently, Uber has a market share of just over 50% in San Francisco, Waymo has 27%, and Lyft has 21%. Uber’s market share fell from 66% to around 55% by the end of 2024. Lyft’s market share fell from 34% to around 22% by the end of 2024. (The Last Drivers License Holder)

The total available market (TAM) for autonomous ride-hailing is the total transportation market. However, the market opportunity over the next five years is focused on the served available market (SAM), primarily the ridesharing market in metropolitan regions where ARS is offered.

So, my estimate is that the combined ridesharing and autonomous ride-hailing services (ARS) market will grow 20% to 25% annually, reaching $175-$200 billion by 2030. I forecast that ARS will have 30% to 40% of this combined market by 2030. This would result in approximately $50 billion at the low end of the range and $80 billion at the high end. In any case, this demonstrates a significant market opportunity over the next five years.

Toward the end of those five years, ARS will continue to grow and penetrate that combined market, and also serve an even larger portion of the total available market. For example, it will begin to replace airline flights on short routes such as Dallas to Austin and Boston to New York. So, beyond 2030, I believe the potential for market growth is almost unlimited.

AV Investment Opportunities

Now we can get into the specific investment opportunities for ARS providers, company by company. I’ll explain these in my rank order of expected success. I believe the technology and business models are already proven, with Waymo providing 500,000 weekly paid driverless trips, and what matters most now is deployment and success. Only one company has truly established itself and is the clear leader.

Waymo: The Clear Leader

Waymo, owned by Alphabet (GOOG), is clearly the leader in autonomous ride-hailing services today. It has rapidly grown to 500,000 paid driverless trips per week. The rapid increase is illustrated in the following chart from TechCrunch.

The explosive growth is highly leveraged by three factors. Once Waymo is established in a metropolitan area, it can rapidly expand its service more broadly by enlarging its geofenced area. It also increases adoption rates as more people become comfortable with the new autonomous service. Finally, it expands its footprint by expanding into additional metropolitan areas, and this is where it gets exciting.

Waymo is currently providing autonomous ride-hailing in 10 metropolitan areas and preparing to expand to 21 more (Source: Waymo app and Autonomous Vehicles: Opportunities, Strategies, and Disruptions), as is explained in the following chart.

Its expansion strategy has four characteristics:

  • It clusters in regions within states like Texas and Florida to improve efficiency. This will also enable it to provide city-to-city services, such as Miami to Orlando or Tampa.
  • It is using some partnerships, such as Uber and Moove, to expand faster.
  • It is phasing its expansion, starting with more friendly Sunbelt states.
  • At the same time, it is testing a global vision with diverse landscapes and regulations.

I project that Waymo will complete as many as 40 million trips this year, based on already being at 500,000 per week and estimating 1 million per week by the end of the year. This would generate approximately $800 million in revenue this year. Using the average of $65 billion in my previous market estimate range for 2030, and assuming that Waymo maintains its leadership position with 50% market share, it would have $32 billion in ARS revenue in 2030.

From an investment standpoint, Waymo is complex. It is a majority owned subsidiary of Alphabet. Although it does not disclose its ownership percentage, I believe that it is approximately 80%-85%. Recently, Waymo raised $16 billion at a post-money valuation of $126 billion. With the estimated 80% ownership, this is currently worth more than $100 billion to Alphabet. Given its $3.5 trillion market cap, it's difficult to estimate how Waymo's valuation will change Alphabet's. Waymo would need to be worth about $1 trillion or more to significantly increase Alphabet's value, but I believe this is achievable. So, I rate it as a Buy based on Waymo's potential. There is also a good chance it will spin off Waymo, as the outside investors who have put in billions will want to monetize their investments.

Zoox: The Purpose-Built Challenger

I currently rate Zoox as number 2 on my ARS list. Like Waymo, Zoox is also a subsidiary of a much larger company, in this case, Amazon (AMZN).

Zoox recently announced the expansion of testing in Dallas and Phoenix, increasing its planned ARS service to ten metropolitan areas. Existing metropolitan areas it plans to enter include Las Vegas, the San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle, Austin, Miami, Los Angeles, and Washington, D.C. Currently, it provides service to Las Vegas and San Francisco. It is also building a facility in Scottsdale to serve as a command hub for fleet operations.

Zoox plans to use an autonomous vehicle (AV) designed specifically for ride-hailing. It has no driver controls, creating a passenger-friendly cabin with four seats facing each other. It has four-wheel steering, making it directional, and is equipped with a 360-degree view using cameras, radar, and lidar. Currently, Zoox is approved for demonstration purposes only and is awaiting NHTSA approval to obtain an exemption to deploy 2,500 AVs annually without driver controls for its commercial ARS service. Once approved, the company expects to build and deploy these vehicles quickly. Meanwhile, it is using a modified Toyota Highlander for early testing and limited deployment.

One factor contributing to Zoox's second-place ranking is its 220,000-square-foot production facility in Haywood, California, which can produce 10,000 AVs annually. This, along with its early deployment plans, demonstrates a clear strategic commitment to a significant ARS deployment in the coming years.

As part of Amazon, which has a $2.2 trillion market cap, it's difficult to value Zoox. Amazon paid $1.2 billion to acquire Zoox and has most likely invested billions more, but it is not reflected on its balance sheet or, most likely, in its valuation. I think it definitely provides some upside to Amazon over the next five years, just as its previous investment in AWS.

Uber: A Ride-Request and Dispatch Play

Uber has initiated an interesting mult-partnership approach to autonomous ride-hailing, focusing almost exclusively on the ride-request and dispatch layer of the ARS platform described previously. It does not have its own autonomous driving systems or even its own vehicles. Instead, it is leveraging its proven user platform as it displaces human drivers across its network. Uber's strategy is summarized in the following chart prepared from Uber Press Releases.

The core of this strategy is its commercial deployment partners: Waymo, Motional, Zoox, Apollo Go, and WeRide. The Waymo partnership appears to be a pilot rather than a long-term partnership. Of Waymo’s 10+ metropolitan areas, the Uber-Waymo partnership is only in Austin and Atlanta. In those two locations, customers book rides with Uber, and Waymo provides the rest. The terms or length of this partnership have not been disclosed. Most likely, Uber will receive a portion of the booking fee, as it does with its human drivers. Uber has a similar partnership with Motional in Las Vegas and with Zoox in Las Vegas and Los Angeles. Internationally, Uber has partnerships with Apollo Go in Dubai and WeRide in Abu Dhabi.

I believe the more substantial part of its strategy is its broader platform partnerships. With Lucid/Nuro, Rivian, and BYD. Uber invested heavily in Nuro, Lucid (LCID), and Rivian (RIVN) as part of its agreement to deploy autonomous ridesharing vehicles on the Uber network. In the Lucid partnership, Lucid provides the vehicles, Nuro provides the autonomous driving technology, and Uber provides the ride request and dispatch functions. They have announced their intention to deploy 20,000 or more vehicles. In the Rivian partnership, they announced their intention to deploy up to 50,000 Rivian robotaxi vehicles.

The partnership with NVIDIA is less clear but holds much potential. Recently, the two companies announced the intention to deploy a global fleet of NVIDIA software-driven autonomous vehicles across 28 cities by 2028. What is not clear is whether this already includes the previous partnership plans or is incremental.

Uber’s unique strategy comes with both benefits and risks. It clearly leverages what it does best with its Uber app and extensive customer base. It also gives customers the flexibility to choose either a human driver or a robotaxi. Another advantage is that it stays asset-light. avoiding the extent of capital intensity of the ARS business by having others provide the fleet. Although Uber has invested a few billion dollars in the companies building these vehicles for it. The primary risk is that it only gets a share of the booking, and it’s unclear what that percentage will be. Most likely, it’s like what it currently gets. This could be a problem if autonomous ride-hailing fares decline as they are expected to.

As an investment, Uber has some potential upside but also some risks and limitations. With a market cap of less than $150 billion, there could be upside to the valuation. However, most of Uber’s autonomous ride-hailing revenue will come at the expense of its current business, which it is cannibalizing. Its ARS strategy is more defensive than growth-oriented. Uber will need to capture a significant share of the combined ridesharing and autonomous ride-hailing markets in order to preserve its current ridesharing revenue.

Motional: Lagging Behind

I once had high hopes for Motional, but its primary investors eventually gave up due to the immense capital requirements. Motional is majority-owned by Hyundai Motor Group, with a minority interest held by Aptiv (APTV).

Originally formed in March of 2020, Motional was a true 50/50 joint venture valued at $4 billion. Hyundai contributed $1.6 billion in cash and $0.4 billion in engineering services, while Aptiv contributed its autonomous driving technology. However, as it became more apparent that significant additional capital would be needed, the company did a major restructuring, trying to survive in 2024-2025. Hyundai contributed the necessary capital, and Aptiv passed, reducing its equity position to approximately 15%. The company scaled back its strategy to shift to a more AI-centric approach. Recently, it returned to commercial deployment through an ARS partnership with Uber in Las Vegas, using its IONIQ 5 robotaxi platform. It is now driving autonomously there with safety drivers and plans to expand into other metropolitan areas, most likely in 2027, by partnering with Uber or others.

While Motional may be a competitor in the ARS market, it is too far behind others to be considered an eventual leader.

Investing in Hyundai Motors Group is difficult and risky. Hyundai Motor Company’s primary listing is on the Korea Exchange under ticker 005380. In the U.S., Hyundai trades over‑the‑counter under the symbols HYMTF and HYMLF, but it is difficult to purchase and own.

Tesla: The Elephant in the Room

And finally, let's discuss the "elephant in the room", Tesla. As my followers know, I'm critical of Tesla's robotaxi strategy. See Without Valuing Future Products, What Is Tesla Really Worth?

I'm high on Tesla's autonomous driving technology. I've owned three and drive the latest versions of FSD. It's by far the best Level 2 system, possibly Level 3, although it is not approved for that and has a long way to go to get approvals.

My objection is to its robotaxi strategy for the following reasons:

1. Tesla’s Robotaxi requires Level 5, not Level 4 capability.

Fully-autonomous (Level 5) driving is required for its robotaxi. Returning to the earlier definitions of autonomous driving, Tesla's FSD is at best significantly autonomous, Level 3. However, for its robotaxi strategy of driving everywhere, it must be fully-autonomous. As I previously explained, achieving full autonomy is still not feasible. And may not be for very long.

It doesn't matter how well Tesla FSD manages various traffic situations. It already performs excellently and will continue to improve. The limitation of fully-autonomous driving pertains to different driving conditions and environments. It must be 100.0 percent, not 99.9 percent, because it intends to provide unconstrained services.

2. Tesla plans to serve an unconstrained area.

It plans an unconstrained service area. Tesla does not intend for its robotaxi service to be geofenced, and its technology platform does not currently include HD maps to facilitate geofencing. Elon Musk has often criticized geofencing strategies as not scalable and has stated that Tesla aims to deploy its robotaxis on a larger scale. Musk has asserted that geographic boundaries will not constrain Tesla. Envision what would happen when it got to the gate of a gated community.

3. Tesla doesn’t have the right business model for its Robotaxi service.

It doesn't have the right business model. Tesla's strategy employs the wrong business model for autonomous ridehailing services, in my opinion. The appropriate model utilizes sufficiently-autonomous driving technology, which, by definition, involves operating within a constrained area. The ideal business model involves a fleet of sufficiently-autonomous vehicles operating within a defined metropolitan area, managed by a municipal operations center.

4. Tesla’s customer-owned fleet strategy won’t work.

The Cybercab faces limitations and is unlikely to succeed as a personal autonomous vehicle, in my view. Its design restricts it to only two passengers, which will significantly limit the market. Additionally, inductive charging is a drawback because it cannot use Tesla's extensive charging network. However, most importantly, without driver controls, it likely won't be able to do some simple things like correctly park in a garage, double park in a driveway, or park in the assigned parking spot.

5. Tesla seems to be ignoring the need for government regulations.

Government regulations could pose an insurmountable challenge. Tesla cannot release its cybercab vehicles to operate in any US city without approval from that jurisdiction. Regulations for autonomous ridehailing services are broad, complex, and varied. Its plan for a nationwide rollout would need approval from all states, which is a tough, if not impossible, task. There are local regulations. Municipalities have the authority to control traffic, limit use, and issue permits for pick-up and drop-off zones. Most even directly regulate autonomous vehicles. Finally, locations such as airports require specific approvals. For example, Waymo is still working on getting approval for trips at the Austin airport. Tesla appears to be simply ignoring the need to get these approvals.

So far, I believe Tesla has grossly failed to live up to its robotaxi predictions. According to Electrek, as of March 16, 2026:

Based on the latest crowdsourced data from independent trackers who reverse-engineered Tesla’s ride-hailing app, the company is operating approximately 35 vehicles in Austin, nine months after launch. Availability remains low — our status check last month found it below 20%.

Every single one of those vehicles operates with an in-car safety supervisor who has a finger on a kill switch. That supervisor is presumably preventing additional crashes beyond what we’re seeing in the NHTSA data — making the actual per-mile crash rate of the unsupervised autonomous system even worse than the reported numbers suggest.

Tesla is also operating a single unsupervised robotaxi that is limited to a small section of the service area in Austin. Musk announced in January that Tesla had started offering rides without a safety monitor, but the “unsupervised” fleet is limited to a single vehicle operating at a time in a very limited part of the Austin service area.

I believe that Tesla will be forced to follow the ARS deployment model, proven successful by other companies, especially Waymo. It will need to deploy the service by metropolitan area, obtain approvals from state and local authorities, geofence routes and destinations until expansion is proven, and provide the service with a Tesla-owned robotaxi fleet. This means that its robotaxi service will be well behind Waymo's, take many years to meet the expectations Musk set, and be extremely expensive, requiring billions in capital investment.

From an investment standpoint, I continue to believe that Tesla is way overvalued at $1.3 trillion. (See: Without Valuing Future Products, What Is Tesla Really Worth?) As discussed in that article, most of its valuation, as much as one trillion dollars, is based on projections for its robotaxi business and humanoid robots. So, in the best (I believe unrealistic) case, a huge robotaxi success is already priced in. In the worst case, there is a 70% to 80% downside risk, as computed in the previous article.

Overall Investment Conclusions

As we start our journey through the AV Investment Roadmap, we see that the most significant transformation in transportation is underway, with autonomous ride-hailing services replacing ridesharing and creating an even larger total available market. However, investment opportunities here are somewhat restricted.

  • Waymo (Alphabet) is clearly the leader, and I project it will be very successful. It may even be worth a trillion dollars over the next five years or so, but even that would be only about a third of Alphabet's current valuation. Nevertheless, it's another reason that Alphabet is a Buy.
  • Zoox is in a similar situation to Amazon as a subsidiary. As the company is in second place in my rankings, if it grew to be worth $500 billion, it would be only 20% of Amazon's value today. However, again, this is a strong supporting reason to buy Amazon.
  • I think Uber's autonomous ride-hailing strategy is the best for it. However, even if it's successful, it will cannibalize its current ridesharing business, so it's unlikely to deliver significant returns to investors. I classify it as a Hold.
  • Motional may have some limited success, but it's a difficult investment.
  • Rivian and Lucid are worth watching to see if these partnerships are positive for them.
  • As for Tesla, it's way overvalued with unrealistic robotaxi expectations. There is no upside, but a substantial downside. For me, it's clearly a Sell and possibly a stock to short over the longer term.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Quantifying The Impact Of The New CMS Payment Increase
data.description:
I have a strong stomach that helps me weather stock market volatility. Maybe the extensive investment time horizon I enjoy plays a big role here too. But I would be lying if I said I have not doubted myself a couple of times over the past year with my UnitedHealth Group (UNH) investment. Almost a year ago, I thought the market was wrong about UNH. Since then, the stock has lost almost a third of its value. A sucker punch by Mr. Market for doubting his valuation of the company.

Last November, I doubled down on my bullish stance on UNH and thought investors should jump on board before buybacks resume.

Some of the projections that I highlighted in my original thesis have not come true. For instance, I expected increased utilization levels to normalize as the demand for elective procedures wanes (many patients delayed these procedures during Covid, leading to pent-up demand in later years). This has not happened. Even in Q4 2025, utilization levels remained elevated. This was a major reason behind UNH's lackluster performance last year.

Then came the loss of members. UnitedHealth had no option but to reprice its books. And this has led to a noticeable loss of members. The company now expects to lose at least 1.4 million MA members this year, bringing the total decrease in policies to at least 2.3 million. This is not good news. As a result of these projections, the company has guided for a 2% YoY decrease in revenue this year.

Amid all these negative developments, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services released its 2027 MA rate announcements yesterday (April 6). For 2027, the average rate increase would be 2.48%, which translates to $13 billion in additional MA payments (incorporating risk trends, the average increase comes to almost 5%). UNH stock gained more than 6% in after-hours trading on this news. This was a major surprise for investors who were expecting a meager 0.09% rate increase based on the advanced notice released by the CMS in January.

In this analysis, I will try to quantify how this rate increase will impact UnitedHealth's financial performance.

Understanding The Impact Of CMS Payment Rate Increase

For 2027, we are now looking at an average rate increase of 2.48%. If you want to fully understand the rationale behind this payment increase, I highly recommend reading the full announcement by CMS, which you can find here.

From a purely financial performance perspective, this payment increase will not have any impact on UnitedHealth this year. However, given that the company is expected to submit its MA bids for 2027 by the end of June this year, we are likely to see management's projections for 2027 margins and growth in a few months. In upcoming earnings calls, UNH management is likely to reveal their projections for these key performance metrics. Therefore, I believe the CMS payment increase for 2027 will have an impact on UnitedHealth's market valuation well before we see any tangible financial benefits.

Coming back to the financial impact, let me first establish the fact that UNH is facing a profitability crisis. Despite topline growth, the company saw its earnings from operations decline 41% YoY in 2025 due to increased costs.

UnitedHealth 2025 performance

The CMS payment increase will help UNH bridge the gap between surging costs and premiums. Also, the company is seeing a massive loss of members due to its repricing strategy, where UNH has cut back on member benefits. The CMS payment increase should help UNH reverse some of this impact, as the company would be in a position to lure new members with more attractive benefit packages. For 2026, the company has decided to secure margins at the cost of growth. Although this sounds like a prudent strategy, UNH cannot afford to lose market share to competitors indefinitely. I believe the CMS payment increase allows the company to be more aggressive with its pricing and, therefore, retain or even grow its market share in 2027.

To better understand the expected financial impact of the CMS payment increase, we can use a simple calculation to single out the positive surprise resulting from the announcement.

Metric Value
Projected MA members by the end of 2026 7.27 million (midpoint of the management guidance)
Projected Medicare and Retirement revenue in 2026 $165 billion
Average annual revenue per MA member $22,696 ($165 billion/7.27 million)
Rate delta 2.39% (2.48%-0.09%)
Incremental revenue potential in 2027 $3.94 billion ($165 billion x 2.39%)
Estimated flow-through rate to operating income 25%
Estimated incremental operating profit boost $985 million

Source: Author's estimates, management guidance

From a total revenue perspective, an incremental $3.94 billion may not look significant given that UNH is expected to report revenue of close to $440 billion this year. However, the real highlight is how the CMS payment increase will boost operating earnings. The company expects operating earnings of at least $24 billion this year. The CMS payment increase, as shown above, can be expected to boost the operating income by an incremental $985 million, or by over 4%. This is assuming a 25% flow-through to operating income, which is a very conservative estimate.

I used a 25% flow-through rate, as I believe UNH will direct the majority of this revenue windfall to win back lost members. This, in my opinion, will be the best use case for the incremental revenue expected from the payment increase. When UNH submits its bids for 2027 in a few months, the company will have full visibility into the ~$3 billion revenue windfall. This will help the company bid aggressively to recapture some lost market share. UnitedHealth was forced to cut back on supplemental benefits this year to avoid an erosion in profit margins. With the revenue windfall from the CMS payment increase expected to hit next year, UNH can be more aggressive in offering OTC allowances and even including dental, vision, and hearing benefits to payers next year. These bids will be submitted to the CMS by June, as I highlighted earlier. The potential addition of supplemental benefits should help UNH lure new customers.

There is also the possibility that UNH will use some of this revenue windfall to cautiously enter new markets and revive its expansion strategy that was put on hold last year as part of its efforts to secure margins. This is because some of these new markets proved to be unprofitable. From a long-term growth perspective, market expansion is essential to ensure sustainable earnings growth.

On the back of these developments and expectations, I am confident in maintaining my bullish stance on UnitedHealth for a few reasons.

  1. I believe the demand for elective healthcare procedures will eventually normalize.
  2. UNH's decision to eliminate unprofitable businesses will boost margins.
  3. With the debt-to-capital ratio inching closer to management's target, a resumption of buybacks looks to be on the cards for H2 2026.
  4. Earnings growth will accelerate beyond 2027 as the company exits 2026 as a leaner business with a focus on profitable segments.

Risks

I am keeping an eye on a few risks that threaten my investment thesis, starting with the uncertain regulatory environment. UNH has been subject to increased regulatory volatility in the past couple of years, and I don't see this environment improving materially in the coming months. Any adverse developments could trigger another market selloff.

Persistently elevated reimbursement costs will hurt margins. Given that I expect the demand for elective healthcare procedures to gradually decrease in the coming quarters, I am keeping an eye on these trends for data that suggests the opposite will happen.

Takeaway

UnitedHealth has been battered for a couple of years, but the tide is finally turning in its favor with the 2027 CMS payment increase. With earnings growth set to revive in 2027, I am thinking of accumulating more UNH shares in the coming weeks. The next couple of earnings calls will provide valuable insights as to how the company is planning to capture some lost market share by offering attractive benefits packages. This could act as a catalyst to drive the share price higher well before UNH reaps the tangible financial rewards of the CMS payment increase.
